original_abstract:
In this study, we evaluate the potential of diffusion probabilistic models for generating high-quality three-dimensional (3D) medical data, such as CT and MRI scans. While generative models have seen significant success in 2D computer vision, their systematic application to 3D medical volumes remains limited despite their promise for privacy-preserving data sharing and dataset augmentation. 

Our contributions are fivefold: (i) We propose a novel architecture that generates high-resolution 3D images by learning the underlying structure of a VQ-GAN latent space, significantly reducing the computational overhead typically associated with 3D diffusion. (ii) We demonstrate the versatility of this method across four public datasets covering diverse modalities (CT and MRI) and anatomical regions (brain, chest, breast, and knee), maintaining performance even with small sample sizes (n ≤ 1844). (iii) We show that our model outperforms established 3D GAN benchmarks by producing more diverse images and avoiding the common pitfall of mode collapse. (iv) Recognizing that standard automated metrics often fail to capture clinical nuances, we validated our results through a rigorous qualitative assessment by experienced radiologists, who confirmed the realism and anatomical consistency of the generated volumes. (v) Finally, we demonstrate the practical utility of our synthetic data in low-data regimes. By pre-training a Swin-UNETR model in a self-supervised manner on synthetic images from one institution, we achieved a substantial performance boost in downstream segmentation tasks at other institutions. For instance, in breast MRI segmentation with only 5% available labeled data, Dice scores improved from 0.91 to 0.95. These results suggest that latent diffusion models are a powerful tool for overcoming data scarcity in medical imaging. We have made our code publicly available to facilitate further research in the field.
humanized_abstract:
In this work we investigate the use of diffusion probabilistic models for 3D medical data synthesis, specifically for CT and MRI scans. Generative models have been widely explored in 2D computer vision with great success, however, the systematic application of 3D generative models to medical data is very limited. Medical imaging data in 3D are of great importance and could be widely used in many applications such as: data augmentation, synthetic data generation for training deep learning models, and privacy-preserving data sharing.

Our contributions are as follows: (i) A new model is proposed for fast 3D image synthesis in the VQ-GAN latent space. The generated 3D images are of high resolution, while the corresponding 3D diffusion processing is significantly speeded up. (ii) The proposed method is evaluated with comprehensive experiments on four open-source medical imaging datasets, namely brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI, at various scanner institutions, and different anatomical regions, even when the training set has very few samples, down to n ≤ 1844. (iii) The 3D3D-GAN demonstrates higher image synthesis quality and larger output image diversity than other 3D image generators based on GANs. (iv) Despite the good evaluation scores obtained by 3D3D-GAN by the common evaluation metrics, as discussed in the previous section, real radiologists judge that the synthetic medical volumes produced by 3D3D-GAN are very real and physiologically consistent. (v) In addition, to demonstrate the value of the synthetic training data (the training set produced with our proposed 3D3D-GAN), we train a pre-trained 3D Swin-UNETR model in a self-supervised fashion on the 3D synthetic data from one site and evaluate the model on 3D segmentation tasks from other institutions. By experimenting with the breast MRI dataset with as little as only 5% of the whole data marked and which gives a training dataset of only 84 samples with less than 10% with manual segmentations large than 1000 voxels, we obtain segmentation Dice scores of 0.91 for a 3D U-Net and of 0.95 for a 3D Swin-UNETR model. Hence, latent diffusion models are good tools to deal with the data-scarity challenges that are inherent in 3D medical image segmentation tasks. Our code is released to the community for the further researches in this very important field of computer science.

document.output:
In this work we investigate the use of diffusion probabilistic models for 3D medical data synthesis, specifically for CT and MRI scans. Generative models have been widely explored in 2D computer vision with great success, however, the systematic application of 3D generative models to medical data is very limited. Medical imaging data in 3D are of great importance and could be widely used in many applications such as: data augmentation, synthetic data generation for training deep learning models, and privacy-preserving data sharing.

Our contributions are as follows: (i) A new model is proposed for fast 3D image synthesis in the VQ-GAN latent space. The generated 3D images are of high resolution, while the corresponding 3D diffusion processing is significantly speeded up. (ii) The proposed method is evaluated with comprehensive experiments on four open-source medical imaging datasets, namely brain MRI, chest CT, breast MRI, and knee MRI, at various scanner institutions, and different anatomical regions, even when the training set has very few samples, down to n ≤ 1844. (iii) The 3D3D-GAN demonstrates higher image synthesis quality and larger output image diversity than other 3D image generators based on GANs. (iv) Despite the good evaluation scores obtained by 3D3D-GAN by the common evaluation metrics, as discussed in the previous section, real radiologists judge that the synthetic medical volumes produced by 3D3D-GAN are very real and physiologically consistent. (v) In addition, to demonstrate the value of the synthetic training data (the training set produced with our proposed 3D3D-GAN), we train a pre-trained 3D Swin-UNETR model in a self-supervised fashion on the 3D synthetic data from one site and evaluate the model on 3D segmentation tasks from other institutions. By experimenting with the breast MRI dataset with as little as only 5% of the whole data marked and which gives a training dataset of only 84 samples with less than 10% with manual segmentations large than 1000 voxels, we obtain segmentation Dice scores of 0.91 for a 3D U-Net and of 0.95 for a 3D Swin-UNETR model. Hence, latent diffusion models are good tools to deal with the data-scarity challenges that are inherent in 3D medical image segmentation tasks. Our code is released to the community for the further researches in this very important field of computer science.
document.input:
In this study, we evaluate the potential of diffusion probabilistic models for generating high-quality three-dimensional (3D) medical data, such as CT and MRI scans. While generative models have seen significant success in 2D computer vision, their systematic application to 3D medical volumes remains limited despite their promise for privacy-preserving data sharing and dataset augmentation. 

Our contributions are fivefold: (i) We propose a novel architecture that generates high-resolution 3D images by learning the underlying structure of a VQ-GAN latent space, significantly reducing the computational overhead typically associated with 3D diffusion. (ii) We demonstrate the versatility of this method across four public datasets covering diverse modalities (CT and MRI) and anatomical regions (brain, chest, breast, and knee), maintaining performance even with small sample sizes (n ≤ 1844). (iii) We show that our model outperforms established 3D GAN benchmarks by producing more diverse images and avoiding the common pitfall of mode collapse. (iv) Recognizing that standard automated metrics often fail to capture clinical nuances, we validated our results through a rigorous qualitative assessment by experienced radiologists, who confirmed the realism and anatomical consistency of the generated volumes. (v) Finally, we demonstrate the practical utility of our synthetic data in low-data regimes. By pre-training a Swin-UNETR model in a self-supervised manner on synthetic images from one institution, we achieved a substantial performance boost in downstream segmentation tasks at other institutions. For instance, in breast MRI segmentation with only 5% available labeled data, Dice scores improved from 0.91 to 0.95. These results suggest that latent diffusion models are a powerful tool for overcoming data scarcity in medical imaging. We have made our code publicly available to facilitate further research in the field.
